date_or_window: No fixed date or window is currently stated by MAS for the stablecoin bill's introduction, enactment or entry into force. MAS's most recent official statement on timing -- Managing Director Chia Der Jiun's remarks at the Singapore FinTech Festival on 13 November 2025 -- states only that MAS 'has finalised the features of our stablecoin regulatory regime and will be preparing draft legislation', giving no date. This supersedes, without repeating, an earlier officially-stated near-term window: a 24 September 2025 written parliamentary reply in which MAS said it 'will issue a public consultation later this year' (i.e. by end-2025) on the legislative amendments. This research found no evidence that consultation was issued, and live-confirmed via Singapore Parliament's own 'Bills Introduced' listing, fetched directly today (13 July 2026), that no Payment Services Act, Financial Services and Markets Act, or stablecoin-titled bill has yet been introduced (most recently introduced bill: Bill No. 14/2026, a Road Traffic amendment bill, introduced 7 July 2026, unrelated)
event: MAS's dedicated single-currency stablecoin (SCS) regulatory framework, finalised in design on 15 August 2023, still awaits the legislative amendments needed to give it legal effect. MAS Managing Director Chia Der Jiun told the Singapore FinTech Festival on 13 November 2025: 'MAS recognises this, and has finalised the features of our stablecoin regulatory regime and will be preparing draft legislation. Under our regime, we have given importance to sound reserve backing and redemption reliability' -- MAS's most recent official statement on this bill, and one that gives no date for introduction, enactment or commencement. Text confirmed via the Bank for International Settlements' own central-bank-speech mirror of this address (bis.org/review/r251117e.htm, titled 'Chia Der Jiun: Creating the future of finance - a journey of innovation and collaboration'), since mas.gov.sg itself served an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page on direct fetch, live-verified in this research session today. This statement follows, but notably does not repeat, an earlier and more specific officially-stated window: in a 24 September 2025 written parliamentary reply on the impact of the US GENIUS Act on Singapore's competitiveness (answered by then-MAS Chairman Gan Kim Yong), MAS stated: 'MAS is working on legislative amendments to formalise the framework, and will issue a public consultation later this year' -- text likewise confirmed via a Wayback Machine snapshot of the mas.gov.sg parliamentary-reply page, since the live page is identically blocked. This research found no MAS consultation paper on stablecoin legislative amendments published in the remainder of 2025 (via direct fetch or Wayback indexing of mas.gov.sg's consultations listing), so that 'later this year' target appears, on the evidence available this session, not to have been met. As of this card's generation date, this research live-fetched Singapore Parliament's own 'Bills Introduced' listing (parliament.gov.sg, not blocked) and confirmed no Payment Services Act, Financial Services and Markets Act, or stablecoin-titled bill appears among the ten most recently introduced bills as of 13 July 2026

date_or_window: 'in 2026' -- stated verbatim as 'More details of this future trial will be shared in 2026' in MAS's 13 November 2025 media release, and consistently as 'We will release more details on this next year' in MD Chia Der Jiun's Singapore FinTech Festival remarks the same day; no quarter or more specific date is given in either source, and this research found no subsequent MAS primary release narrowing or fulfilling that window between 13 November 2025 and this card's 13 July 2026 generation date
event: Building on the first live issuance of Singapore-dollar wholesale central bank digital currency (CBDC), announced 13 November 2025 -- a trial in which DBS, OCBC and UOB settled interbank overnight-lending transactions on the Singapore Dollar Test Network (SGD Testnet) -- MAS's own media release states verbatim: 'MAS will build on this CBDC settlement pilot, and plans in a future trial, to issue tokenised MAS Bills to Primary Dealers which will be settled using CBDC. More details of this future trial will be shared in 2026.' Confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF today via the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg), since mas.gov.sg itself served an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page on every path tried, live-verified in this research session. MD Chia Der Jiun's remarks at the Singapore FinTech Festival, delivered the same day, restate the same plan: 'As the next bound, MAS will trial the issuance of tokenised MAS Bills to Primary Dealers and settled with CBDC. We will release more details on this next year' -- confirmed via a Wayback Machine snapshot of MAS's speech page, since the live mas.gov.sg page is identically blocked. As of this card's generation date (13 July 2026), this research found no MAS primary release -- via direct fetch or Wayback indexing of mas.gov.sg's 2026 media-releases index -- narrowing this 'in 2026' window to a specific date or reporting that the tokenised MAS Bills trial has occurred

date_or_window: Consultation published 10 June 2026; closes for public feedback 31 July 2026 at 11.30pm. Once finalised, the amended Notices are stated to take effect 12 months after the finalised Notice is published -- an officially-described window pegged to a not-yet-fixed publication date, not itself a fixed calendar date
event: MAS published a Consultation Paper on Proposed Amendments to Notices on Technology Risk Management on 10 June 2026, covering six Notices spanning banking, insurance, payments and capital markets -- among them Notice FSM-N13, the Technology Risk Management notice applicable to digital payment token (DPT) service providers -- proposing changes to IT asset management, IT risk assessment and monitoring, capacity planning, change management controls, continuous system and security monitoring, immutable/offline data backup, and incident management requirements. The consultation closes for public feedback on 31 July 2026 at 11.30pm, and affected financial institutions, including DPT service providers, are stated to have a 12-month transition period to implement the finalised requirements once published. This research could not directly fetch or quote MAS's own consultation paper: both the live mas.gov.sg page and a Wayback Machine snapshot of it (captured 19 June 2026) returned the identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page served to every mas.gov.sg path tested throughout this research session, live-verified today. The publication date, closing date, DPT/FSM-N13 scope, and 12-month transition period stated here therefore rest on secondary corroboration only -- consistent, independent compliance-industry summaries (Waystone Compliance; Baker McKenzie) reporting the same closing date, transition period, and DPT/FSM-N13 coverage -- not on independently read primary text; this should be re-verified against MAS's own consultation paper directly once mas.gov.sg is fetchable
